title: Interview Templates
description: Master the five most common interview algorithm templates in C#: sliding window, binary search, two-pointer, prefix sum, and backtracking.

sections.title: Sliding Window
sections.explanation: Sliding window maintains a window [left, right] that expands and contracts over an array/string. Two variants: (1) Fixed-size: advance both pointers together. (2) Variable-size: expand right, shrink left when condition is violated. Used for max/min subarray, longest substring without repeats, minimum window substring. Time: O(n) since each element enters and leaves the window once.

exercise.prompt: Implement a fixed-size sliding window: given int[] nums = {1, 3, 2, 6, -1, 4, 1, 8, 2} and k=5, find the maximum average of any subarray of size k. Print the maximum average as a double.

validation.message: Use the sliding window add-right-remove-left pattern with Math.Max.

exercise.hint: Compute initial window sum for first k elements. Slide: add nums[i], subtract nums[i-k]. Track max sum, return maxSum / k.

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.explanation: Binary search finds a target in O(log n) on sorted data. Template: left=0, right=length-1. While left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)/2 (avoids overflow). If found, return mid. If target > nums[mid], left = mid+1. Else right = mid-1. Variants: find leftmost/rightmost occurrence, search for insertion point, search on answer space.

exercise.prompt: Implement binary search to find the first position where nums[i] >= target (lower bound). Given sorted array {1, 2, 4, 4, 4, 7, 9}, find the first index where value >= 4, then where value >= 5. Print both indices.

validation.message: First index >= 4 is index 2. First index >= 5 is index 5 (value 7).
Use the standard binary search template with overflow-safe mid calculation.

exercise.hint: while (left < right): mid = left + (right-left)/2. If nums[mid] < target: left = mid+1. Else: right = mid.

sections.title: Two Pointers
sections.explanation: Two pointers use two indices moving toward each other or in the same direction. (1) Opposite ends: start at left=0, right=n-1, move inward (palindrome check, two-sum on sorted array, container with most water). (2) Same direction: slow/fast pointers (remove duplicates, linked list cycle). Typically O(n) time, O(1) space.

exercise.prompt: Use two pointers to remove duplicates from sorted array in-place. Given int[] nums = {1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5}, return the new length of unique elements. Print the new length and the unique elements as an array.

validation.message: Use slow and fast pointers, advancing slow only when a new unique value is found.

exercise.hint: For fast from 1 to end: if nums[fast] != nums[slow], increment slow and copy nums[fast] to nums[slow].

sections.title: Prefix Sum
sections.explanation: Prefix sum array stores cumulative sums: prefix[i] = sum of nums[0..i-1]. This lets you compute any subarray sum in O(1): sum(i..j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. Build prefix array in O(n), then answer range-sum queries instantly. Used in subarray sum equals K (with Dictionary), range queries, and 2D matrix sums.

exercise.prompt: Build a prefix sum array for nums = {3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6}. Use it to compute and print: sum of elements from index 2 to 5 (inclusive), and sum of elements from index 0 to 3 (inclusive).

validation.message: Build prefix array with prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i] and query with subtraction.

exercise.hint: prefix[0]=0. For i in 0..n-1: prefix[i+1]=prefix[i]+nums[i]. Sum(i..j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i].

sections.title: Backtracking Template
sections.explanation: Backtracking systematically explores all candidates, pruning invalid paths early. Template: (1) if solution is complete, add to results. (2) for each candidate: check if valid, add to current path, recurse, remove from path (backtrack). Key optimization: prune early with validity checks before recursing. Used for combinations, permutations, subsets, Sudoku, word search.

exercise.prompt: Use backtracking to generate all subsets of {1, 2, 3}. Each subset is a list. Print all subsets, one per line, using "[items]" format. There should be 8 subsets total (including the empty set). Print the total count at the end.

validation.message: All 8 subsets of {1,2,3} followed by the count 8.
Add a copy of current to result, then for each candidate: add, recurse, remove (backtrack).

exercise.hint: Add current (copy!) to result at every call. Loop i from start to end: add nums[i], recurse with i+1, remove last.
